From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

For the film, see [Minas Conspiracy (film)](/wiki/Minas_Conspiracy_(film) "Minas Conspiracy (film)").

18th century separatist movement in Colonial Brazil

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [icon](/wiki/File:Question_book-new.svg) | This article **needs additional citations for [verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")**. Please help [improve this article](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Inconfid%C3%AAncia_Mineira "Special:EditPage/Inconfidência Mineira") by [adding citations to reliable sources](/wiki/Help:Referencing_for_beginners "Help:Referencing for beginners"). Unsourced material may be challenged and removed. *Find sources:* ["Inconfidência Mineira"](https://www.google.com/search?as_eq=wikipedia&q=%22Inconfid%C3%AAncia+Mineira%22) – [news](https://www.google.com/search?tbm=nws&q=%22Inconfid%C3%AAncia+Mineira%22+-wikipedia&tbs=ar:1) **·** [newspapers](https://www.google.com/search?&q=%22Inconfid%C3%AAncia+Mineira%22&tbs=bkt:s&tbm=bks) **·** [books](https://www.google.com/search?tbs=bks:1&q=%22Inconfid%C3%AAncia+Mineira%22+-wikipedia) **·** [scholar](https://scholar.google.com/scholar?q=%22Inconfid%C3%AAncia+Mineira%22) **·** [JSTOR](https://www.jstor.org/action/doBasicSearch?Query=%22Inconfid%C3%AAncia+Mineira%22&acc=on&wc=on) *(January 2014)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

Minas Gerais Conspiracy

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| Part of the [Atlantic Revolutions](/wiki/Atlantic_Revolutions "Atlantic Revolutions") | |
| *The conspirators' flag*, by Carlos Oswald | |
| Map | |
| Native name | Inconfidência Mineira |
| Date | 1789 |
| Location | Captaincy of Minas Gerais, [State of Brazil](/wiki/State_of_Brazil "State of Brazil") |
| Cause | * Portuguese repression motivated by the exhaustion of the mines * Imminence of the derrama in 1789 |
| Target | Establishment of an independent republic |
| Participants | [Tiradentes](/wiki/Tiradentes "Tiradentes") [Executed](/wiki/Capital_punishment "Executed") Freire de Andrade José Álvares Maciel [Tomás António Gonzaga](/wiki/Tom%C3%A1s_Ant%C3%B3nio_Gonzaga "Tomás António Gonzaga") Maia e Barbalho |
| Outcome | * Revolt denounced before its outbreak * Most of the leaders were exiled * Tiradentes sentenced to death |

The ***Inconfidência Mineira*** (Portuguese pronunciation: [[ĩkõfiˈdẽsiɐ miˈnejɾɐ]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Portuguese "Help:IPA/Portuguese"); "Minas Gerais Conspiracy") was an unsuccessful [separatist movement](/wiki/Separatist_movement "Separatist movement") in [Colonial Brazil](/wiki/Colonial_Brazil "Colonial Brazil") in 1789. It was the result of a confluence of external and internal causes. The external inspiration was the [independence](/wiki/History_of_the_United_States_(1776%E2%80%931789) "History of the United States (1776–1789)") of [thirteen British colonies](/wiki/Thirteen_Colonies "Thirteen Colonies") in North America following the [American Revolutionary War](/wiki/American_Revolutionary_War "American Revolutionary War"), a development that impressed the intellectual elite of particularly the [captaincy](/wiki/Captaincies_of_Brazil "Captaincies of Brazil") of [Minas Gerais](/wiki/Minas_Gerais "Minas Gerais").

The main internal cause of the conspiracy was the decline of gold mining in that captaincy. As gold became less plentiful, the region's gold miners faced increasing difficulties in fulfilling tax obligations to the Portuguese crown (the tax over gold was one-fifth). When the captaincy could not satisfy the royal demand for gold, it was burdened with an additional tax on gold, called *derrama*.

Conspirators seeking independence from Portugal planned to rise up in rebellion on the day that the *derrama* would be instituted.[[1]](#cite_note-1) However, the conspirators lacked both well-formed plans and an overall leader. Some of the conspirators were republicans, others were monarchists. Some favored the abolition of [slavery](/wiki/Slavery_in_Brazil "Slavery in Brazil"), while others deemed abolition as impractical at that time. The conspirators did put forth a few economic and social ideas: the promotion of cotton production, the exploitation of iron and saltpeter reserves, a proposal to give incentives to mothers to have many children, and the creation of a citizens' militia.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d2/Bandeira_da_Inconfid%C3%AAncia_Mineira.svg/250px-Bandeira_da_Inconfid%C3%AAncia_Mineira.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Bandeira_da_Inconfid%C3%AAncia_Mineira.svg)

The flag proposed by the conspirators for the new republic, which became the basis for the current [Flag of Minas Gerais](/wiki/Flag_of_Minas_Gerais "Flag of Minas Gerais"). The inscription in Latin reads "Freedom, albeit late"

The conspiracy attracted a great number of military personnel, priests, and intellectuals, as well as the poets [Cláudio Manuel da Costa](/wiki/Cl%C3%A1udio_Manuel_da_Costa "Cláudio Manuel da Costa") and [Tomás Antônio Gonzaga](/wiki/Tom%C3%A1s_Ant%C3%B3nio_Gonzaga "Tomás António Gonzaga") (1744–1807?). Among the best known participants were [Joaquim José da Silva Xavier](/wiki/Tiradentes "Tiradentes"), best known as "Tiradentes"; José Álvares Maciel, a philosopher and chemistry student; and lieutenant colonel Francisco de Paula Freire de Andrade (1756–1792) of the regiment of dragoons. Tiradentes, who came from Andrade's regiment, was the independence movement's most enthusiastic propagandist.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

## Conspirators

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Inconfid%C3%AAncia_Mineira&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Conspirators")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/14/Resposta_de_Tiradentes.jpg/250px-Resposta_de_Tiradentes.jpg)](/wiki/File:Resposta_de_Tiradentes.jpg)

Response of Joaquim José da Silva Xavier, known as *[Tiradentes](/wiki/Tiradentes "Tiradentes")*, to the commutation of the rebels' punishment

The *Inconfidência* was inspired by the ideals of the [French liberal](/wiki/French_liberalism "French liberalism") philosophers of the [Age of Enlightenment](/wiki/Age_of_Enlightenment "Age of Enlightenment") and the successful [American Revolution](/wiki/American_Revolution "American Revolution").[[3]](#cite_note-3) The conspirators largely belonged to the white upper class of minerals-rich [Minas Gerais](/wiki/Minas_Gerais "Minas Gerais"). Many had studied in Europe, especially in the [University of Coimbra](/wiki/University_of_Coimbra "University of Coimbra"), and some had large debts with the colonial government. In the context of declining gold production, the intention of the Portuguese government to impose the obligatory payment of all debts (the *derrama*) was a leading cause behind the conspiracy. Many of the conspirators wanted to create a [republic](/wiki/Republic "Republic") in which the leader would be chosen through democratic elections. The capital would be [São João del Rei](/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Jo%C3%A3o_del_Rei "São João del Rei"), and [Ouro Preto](/wiki/Ouro_Preto "Ouro Preto") would become a university town. The structure of the society, including the [right to property](/wiki/Right_to_property "Right to property") and the ownership of slaves, would be kept intact.

Eventually, three participants in the independence movement revealed the conspirators' plans to the government, and the rebels were arrested in 1789. Among the movement were the lawyer [Alvarenga Peixoto](/wiki/Alvarenga_Peixoto "Alvarenga Peixoto"), the poets [Tomás Antônio Gonzaga](/wiki/Tom%C3%A1s_Ant%C3%B4nio_Gonzaga "Tomás Antônio Gonzaga") and [Cláudio Manuel da Costa](/wiki/Cl%C3%A1udio_Manuel_da_Costa "Cláudio Manuel da Costa"), the priest José da Silva de Oliveira Rolim, and the *[alferes](/wiki/Alf%C3%A9rez_(rank) "Alférez (rank)")* [Joaquim José da Silva Xavier](/wiki/Joaquim_Jos%C3%A9_da_Silva_Xavier "Joaquim José da Silva Xavier") (a.k.a. "Tiradentes"). After [Joaquim Silvério dos Reis](/wiki/Joaquim_Silv%C3%A9rio_dos_Reis "Joaquim Silvério dos Reis") (1756–1792), a member of the conspiracy, informed on the movement before it could take place, Peixoto was captured, arrested, and sent to [exile](/wiki/Exile "Exile") in the city of [Ambaca](/wiki/Ambaca "Ambaca"), in [Portuguese Angola](/wiki/Portuguese_Angola "Portuguese Angola"), another colony of the [Portuguese Empire](/wiki/Portuguese_Empire "Portuguese Empire"), where he remained until the end of his life.

## Aftermath

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Inconfid%C3%AAncia_Mineira&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Aftermath")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/31/Figueiredo-MHN-Tiradentes.jpg/250px-Figueiredo-MHN-Tiradentes.jpg)](/wiki/File:Figueiredo-MHN-Tiradentes.jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/70/Tiradentes_quartered_%28Tiradentes_escuartejado%29_by_Pedro_Am%C3%A9rico_1893.jpg/250px-Tiradentes_quartered_%28Tiradentes_escuartejado%29_by_Pedro_Am%C3%A9rico_1893.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tiradentes_quartered_(Tiradentes_escuartejado)_by_Pedro_Am%C3%A9rico_1893.jpg)

The execution and quartering of Tiradentes

Judicial proceedings against the conspirators lasted from 1789 to 1792. Lieutenant Colonel Freire de Andrade, [Tiradentes](/wiki/Tiradentes "Tiradentes"), José Álvares Maciel, and eight others were condemned to the [gallows](/wiki/Gallows "Gallows"). Seven more were condemned to perpetual [banishment](/wiki/Banish "Banish") in [Africa](/wiki/Africa "Africa"), the rest were acquitted. Following the trial [Queen Maria I](/wiki/Maria_I_of_Portugal "Maria I of Portugal") [commuted](/wiki/Commutation_of_sentence "Commutation of sentence") the sentences of [capital punishment](/wiki/Capital_punishment "Capital punishment") to perpetual banishment for all except those whose activities involved aggravated circumstances. That was the case for Tiradentes, who took full responsibility for the conspiracy movement and was imprisoned in [Rio de Janeiro](/wiki/Rio_de_Janeiro "Rio de Janeiro"), where he was hanged on 21 April 1792. Afterwards, his body was [torn into pieces](/wiki/Dismemberment "Dismemberment"), which were sent to [Vila Rica](/wiki/Vila_Rica "Vila Rica") in the captaincy of Minas Gerais, to be displayed in the places where he had propagated his revolutionary ideas. The anniversary of his death is celebrated as a [national holiday](/wiki/Public_holidays_in_Brazil "Public holidays in Brazil") in Brazil.

In 1948 the events were portrayed in the film *[Minas Conspiracy](/wiki/Minas_Conspiracy_(film) "Minas Conspiracy (film)")* directed by [Carmen Santos](/wiki/Carmen_Santos "Carmen Santos").

In 1963, Minas Gerais incorporated as its state flag the one designed by the Inconfidência, with an [equilateral triangle](/wiki/Equilateral_triangle "Equilateral triangle") inspired by the [Holy Trinity](/wiki/Holy_Trinity "Holy Trinity") – albeit supposedly the inconfidentes wanted a green triangle, while Minas' flag uses a red one – and a Latin motto taken out of [Vergil](/wiki/Vergil "Vergil")'s *[Eclogues](/wiki/Eclogues "Eclogues")*.[[4]](#cite_note-4)
